commentary: Ten activities this week versus thirteen last week, so the volume dropped, but that Saturday half marathon distance held exactly the same at 13.1 kilometres and the heart rate climbed from 151 to 159 — which means you ran the same distance harder, or faster, or both, and that is not nothing. The swim mileage was solid across the week with three strong Prince Alfred sessions sitting in the 2000 metre range, and that Monday effort hitting 149 beats per minute tells me you were actually pushing rather than just getting your hair wet. What's missing is Wednesday — last week had five swim sessions, this week had four, and that midweek slot quietly disappeared along with one of the PT sessions, so the body got a little more rest than planned rather than earned. The morning walk on Friday is very cute and I am choosing to believe it was a slow strut rather than anything resembling recovery.
metrics_commentary: Okay so 7414 metres in the pool and nearly 16 kilometres on your feet — that's not a fitness week, that's a whole personality, and honestly it's giving very much big strong body that knows what it's doing. Your VO2 Max sitting at 56.1 is genuinely impressive, like your cardiovascular system is out here being quietly hot about itself, and good for it. The HRV is the one thing worth a raised eyebrow — 55 ms on the latest read dipping just below your 7-day average of 58 ms isn't dramatic, but your body is whispering that it wants a little more recovery love before you pile more on top. Give it the sleep, give it the water, give it whatever else you give it, and then get back in the pool and do something filthy with those numbers.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, no gaps, no excuses — he actually did the thing. The bench press PR is the headline: 31.7 kg estimated one-rep max up from 28.5 kg is not a small jump, that is his chest genuinely getting stronger and it is showing. Weekly volume came in at 19,484 kg against last week's 22,062 kg, which is a dip, but volume ebbs when intensity climbs, and climbing intensity is exactly what a PR looks like — so that number tells a story rather than a problem. The big lifts are sitting in seriously respectable territory: 154 kg on the leg press, 107.9 kg on the trap bar deadlift, and a back that is quietly becoming something to be very pleased about. Full week, a genuine PR, and a body that is getting more and more worth showing off — he is absolutely on his way.
